name: marcella frittata
instructions:
A low-heat covered-skillet frittata built around onions + parsley + salted butter, gratinéed with oil-sprayed breadcrumbs at the finish. Marcella Hazan is the source, but this recipe departs from her canon in a few places (see notes on tomatoes, zucchini, and garlic).

FLAVOR BASE (the actual dish): finely dice yellow onion. Cook slowly in a GENEROUS amount of SALTED butter (plus a little olive oil to raise the smoke point) until fully softened and translucent — no browning. This is the load-bearing flavor step. Set the softened onions aside briefly.

BEAT THE EGGS: whisk whole eggs with grated parmesan (careful — see notes), a generous handful of chopped fresh parsley, a small amount of salt (careful — parmesan is already salty), and a HEAVY hand on black pepper (very hard to overdo). Fold in the cooled onions.

COOK — LOW HEAT, COVERED, PULL EARLY:
1. Wipe the pan back out, add another knob of salted butter, and get it just melted over LOW heat.
2. Pour in the egg-and-onion mixture. Cover the skillet with a lid immediately.
3. Keep heat LOW throughout. Do not touch, do not stir, do not lift the lid to peek.
4. Once the pan is genuinely hot (a few minutes in — the eggs will look wet-set at the edges and still liquid on top), PULL THE PAN OFF THE HEAT while keeping it covered. Residual heat under the lid finishes the set from the top down without cooking the bottom past the sweet spot.
5. When the top is just set (no raw liquid pool), you're done with phase one. This whole first phase should feel earlier than you'd expect — the temptation to leave it on the heat 'a bit longer' is exactly the trap that overcooks the base into a rubber disc.

GRATIN FINISH: scatter a generous even layer of PLAIN BREADCRUMBS across the top of the set frittata. Spray the breadcrumbs directly with cooking spray (Pam or equivalent) — this is what gets you the deep golden gratin crust without needing to drizzle oil (which pools and reads uneven). Broil briefly (watch closely — the breadcrumbs go from golden to burnt fast) until the top is deeply browned and crisped.

SERVE: slice into wedges. Works hot, warm, or room temperature. Also works as a scramble if you don't want to bother with the covered-skillet set.
notes:
=== THE GENERAL FORM — WHAT THIS DISH ACTUALLY IS ===

The marcella frittata is fundamentally a slow-cooked-onion + salted-butter + parsley egg base, gratinéed on top with oil-sprayed breadcrumbs. Everything else is optional. Every ingredient you add to the batter increases the moisture load or crowds the onion-butter-parsley flavor — both are things to be defensive about, not things to seek out.

STRUCTURE: (1) flavor base — onions + salted butter + parsley + eggs + parmesan + salt/pepper. (2) low-heat covered set. (3) gratin top with breadcrumbs and cooking spray. Master these three phases before adding anything else.

=== TEMPERATURE CONTROL IS THE WHOLE TECHNIQUE ===

LOW HEAT, COVERED, THE WHOLE TIME: the classic frittata failure is a rubber disc — over-cooked bottom, dry interior. The fix is not high heat with careful watching; the fix is low heat with the lid on and pulling the pan off early. The lid traps steam that cooks the top of the eggs from above at the same time the pan's bottom heat cooks from below. That two-sided gentle heat gives you a tender even set that dry-open-pan-cooking can't.

PULL THE PAN OFF THE HEAT EARLY, KEEPING IT COVERED: once the pan is up to temperature and the frittata is beginning to set at the edges, PULL IT OFF THE BURNER. Leave the lid on. Residual heat in the pan and the trapped steam under the lid finish the top-set without any more direct-flame input from below. If you keep it on the heat until the top LOOKS fully set from outside the lid, the bottom is already overcooked. The pan-off-heat-with-lid-on step is the whole trick. Underdoing this is a much smaller sin than overdoing it — a slightly-underset frittata gets rescued under the broiler during the gratin phase anyway.

NO PEEKING: lifting the lid mid-cook drops the trapped steam and kills the two-sided heat. Trust the covered-pan process.

=== FLAVOR — ONIONS + PARSLEY + SALTED BUTTER (THAT'S IT) ===

THE FLAVOR OF THIS DISH IS ONIONS, PARSLEY, AND SALTED BUTTER. Not eggs. The eggs are the medium; the base flavors are what people taste. Slowly-softened yellow onions in a generous amount of salted butter, plus a heavy hand of fresh parsley, is the whole thing. Everything else (parmesan, pepper, breadcrumbs, secondary vegetables) supports these three or provides texture — but the load-bearing flavor is here.

SALTED BUTTER, ON THE PAN, NON-NEGOTIABLE: butter is not just fat here — it's a primary flavor. Use salted butter (not unsalted) and use a generous amount, both for softening the onions and for the second knob when you cook the eggs. Salted butter's dairy-and-salt combination is exactly what the finished frittata tastes like, and no other fat will replicate it. Olive oil cut in with the butter is fine (raises smoke point during onion softening) but should not carry the fat load.

GARLIC IS NOT USED — UNUSUAL FOR MARCELLA BUT CORRECT HERE: this is one of the few marcella recipes without garlic, which is genuinely unusual for her italian repertoire. It's the right call for this dish. Garlic would fight the onions for the same allium slot and push the flavor toward a heavier onion-garlic-butter combination that reads more like a savory tart filling than a delicate frittata. The onion-and-parsley-alone axis is what makes the marcella frittata distinct. Don't add garlic even if you'd default to it in every other italian egg dish.

PARMESAN AND SALT — NECESSARY BUT BE CAREFUL: both are load-bearing but both compound. Parmesan is salty on its own; salted butter contributes salt; a heavy hand on the salt shaker on top of both leaves you with a frittata that reads more like a salt-and-cheese cracker than a delicate egg dish. Use grated parmesan generously enough to taste it but not so much that it overpowers the onion; use added salt SPARINGLY — remember the butter and the cheese are already contributing. It's easier to add more salt at the table than to fix an over-salted frittata.

BLACK PEPPER IS HARD TO OVERDO — LEAN INTO IT: unlike salt, black pepper is very hard to overdo here. Grind it generously into the egg mixture; the delicate egg base wants a strong pepper backbone to keep it from reading flat. If you find yourself thinking 'that might be too much pepper,' you're probably about right.

IRON SKILLET CAN ADD A FUNKINESS — OPEN QUESTION HOW TO MANAGE: the cast iron skillet contributes a background metallic-funky note to the finished frittata. Not usually enough to ruin the dish but noticeable, and specifically noticeable in this recipe because the base flavors (onion + butter + parsley + egg) are delicate enough not to hide it. Not sure yet how to remove or minimize it. A few hypotheses I haven't tested:
- MORE BUTTER AS A BUFFER: a heavier layer of salted butter between the pan and the egg base might create enough of a fat barrier to prevent the eggs from picking up the iron character. Cheap to try.
- CLEAN THE PAN BETTER FIRST: possibly the funkiness comes from residual polymerized oil or fond from previous cooks that the frittata's low-and-slow cook has time to draw out. Wiping the iron down with salt or a hot-water scrub before starting might help. Also cheap to try.
- USE A STAINLESS STEEL PAN INSTEAD: stainless would obviously avoid the iron problem entirely, but stainless is a much harder pan for this technique — the covered-low-heat set risks the eggs sticking hard to the bottom without a well-seasoned surface. Untested; unclear if the tradeoff is worth it.
Worth working through this systematically at some point — the covered-low-heat technique + delicate onion/butter/parsley base is exactly the kind of dish where a background pan-flavor is not something you can hide behind other ingredients.

=== BREADCRUMB GRATIN FINISH — SPRAY WITH OIL, NOT DRIZZLED ===

BREADCRUMBS ON TOP + COOKING SPRAY: after the frittata is set, scatter a generous even layer of plain breadcrumbs across the top and spray them directly with cooking spray (Pam or equivalent). The spray gives you an even coat of oil across every breadcrumb — enough to crisp them under the broiler without pooling into greasy patches. Drizzling olive oil from a bottle looks fine in theory but always ends up with wet-and-dry patches; the aerosol spray solves this cleanly. Broil until deeply golden — a few minutes, watch closely, they go from golden to burnt fast.

The gratin crust is what makes this feel like a finished dish rather than an omelette. Don't skip it.

=== SECONDARY VEGETABLES — DRY-FILLING ONLY (BELL PEPPERS AND SUN-DRIED TOMATOES) ===

BELL PEPPERS AND SUN-DRIED TOMATOES ARE THE ONLY TWO SECONDARY VEGETABLES I'D REACH FOR — BOTH BECAUSE THEY'RE DRY: the low-heat covered-set only works if the egg batter is dry enough to actually set. Watery vegetables in the filling (tomatoes, zucchini — see failure modes below) trap moisture in the batter, drop the effective cooking temperature at the middle, and force you to leave the pan on heat longer to set the interior — at which point the base is a rubber disc.

- BELL PEPPERS: finely diced and pre-sautéed with the onions (drives off the small amount of water they carry). Add sweetness and a soft vegetal note. Fine, but strictly optional — the recipe works and arguably reads cleaner without them.
- SUN-DRIED TOMATOES: their moisture is already gone (that's what 'sun-dried' means), so they can go directly into the batter without pre-sautéing. Give you a tomato note without the water problem that fresh/canned tomatoes cause (see failure modes). Finely chop before adding.

Neither is required. Both are strictly optional. If you can't think of a specific reason to add a secondary vegetable, don't.

=== CLASHING VEGETABLES — TOO MUCH MOISTURE ===

TOMATOES (FRESH, CANNED, DICED) ARE CLASHING — DESPITE MARCELLA USING THEM IN SOME OF HER FRITTATA RECIPES: marcella puts tomatoes into some of her frittata recipes but I've concluded they're an overall loss. Tomatoes carry too much water; the moist filling makes the INTERIOR cook much slower than the exterior, and to fully set the middle you end up with a hard/dark parmesan crust on the outside and a squishy interior underneath. Neither texture reads well. This is a case where I'm departing from marcella's canon — tomatoes are wrong here even if the source recipe includes them. The frittata format wants dry-cooked vegetables and eggs; anything that drives moisture back into the filling fights the low-heat-even-set technique. Sun-dried tomatoes (secondary — see above) are the exception and the only way to get tomato character into this dish.

ZUCCHINI IS ALSO CLASHING — SAME MOISTURE PROBLEM: zucchini used to be a primary vegetable in this recipe (with deseeding as the workaround). On more testing, even deseeded and sautéed zucchini releases enough water into the frittata batter to cause the same slow-interior / overcooked-exterior problem tomatoes cause. Not worth the workaround; the dish reads better without it. Zucchini as an italian egg vegetable belongs in other recipes (marcella has a whole zucchini-and-egg family in her books), just not in the low-heat covered-set frittata format. Moved to clashing.

GENERAL RULE — ANYTHING THAT RELEASES WATER INTO THE BATTER IS CLASHING: this rule extends beyond tomatoes and zucchini. Mushrooms, spinach, greens, fresh peppers other than bell — all release water when they hit the egg batter and cause the same problem. If you want a wetter vegetable-and-egg dish, cook a scramble instead; the frittata format specifically wants dry.

=== ALSO WORKS AS A SCRAMBLE ===

If you don't want to bother with the covered-skillet set-and-pull-early technique, the same flavor combination works fine as a scramble — soft-cook the onions in salted butter, add beaten eggs with parmesan and parsley, stir gently in the pan until just set. You lose the gratin-top presentation but retain the flavor.
